slides.title: What are Tokens?
slides.content: Tokens are the fundamental unit that AI models use to process text. You can think of tokens as tiny puzzle pieces that models combine to 'understand' and 'generate' content. A token isn't always a full word; it can be a part of a word, a single character, or even whitespace. In the context of Gemini models, the relationship varies based on the text structure and language. A rough heuristic is that 1,000 tokens correspond to approximately 750 words in English. However, for languages with complex writing systems or code, tokenization behaves differently. Input tokens represent the prompt (user data, context, instructions sent to the model), while output tokens constitute the response generated by the model. Both input and output tokens contribute to the total usage and impact the cost and processing time of an API request. Mastering token usage is crucial for building cost-effective, high-performance AI applications, especially when handling large datasets or streaming long-form content. Developers must proactively optimize prompts to minimize token count while maintaining performance and response quality.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#eff6ff'/><rect x='50' y='50' width='80' height='40' rx='5' fill='#3b82f6' fill-opacity='0.1'/><rect x='150' y='50' width='80' height='40' rx='5' fill='#3b82f6' fill-opacity='0.2'/><rect x='250' y='50' width='80' height='40' rx='5' fill='#3b82f6' fill-opacity='0.3'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#1e40af'>Tokens as Modular Data Units</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Explain the high-level concept of tokenization in AI models.
How does the character-to-token ratio change between standard English text and programming code?
Why is it essential for developers to be aware of token limits?

slides.title: Token Calculator & AI Studio
slides.content: Google AI Studio provides built-in tools to estimate and measure token usage in real-time. When you craft a prompt, the environment calculates tokens for the prompt, system instructions, and any uploaded assets or documents. When you run the model, AI Studio tracks the number of tokens used for the input (prompt) and the output (generated response). This feedback loop is essential. For example, if you prompt: 'Summarize the document attached', the token cost includes the entire document length in tokens plus the instruction. Understanding this is key to managing capacity. Furthermore, AI Studio displays performance metrics directly, such as 'Time to First Token' (TTFT) and throughput (tokens/second), allowing you to observe latency firsthand as you iterate. Proactive monitoring in AI Studio ensures that your prompt engineering efforts remain within reasonable constraints and helps identify where overly verbose system instructions might be unnecessarily consuming token capacity without improving output quality linearly.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f0fdfa'/><path d='M100 50 H300 V150 H100 Z' fill='#fff' stroke='#34d399' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M120 70 H280 M120 90 H280 M120 110 H200' stroke='#34d399' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#065f46'>AI Studio Real-time Token Tracking</text></svg>
studentPrompts: How does embedding long context data affect the input token count?
What is the difference between latency, TTFT, and throughput in the context of token consumption?
Describe how to use AI Studio to optimize prompt length effectively.

slides.title: Example Prompt Analysis
slides.content: Let's examine a real-world scenario. Prompt (Input): 'Analyze the following architectural pattern described in the attached file and explain how to improve it for 10x concurrency.' [File content: 2000 tokens]. System Instructions: 'You are an AI architect.' After running this, AI Studio reports: Input: 2050 tokens (2000 for file + 50 for instructions and prompt). Output: 300 tokens generated explaining the architectural improvements. Total Usage: 2350 tokens. Execution Time (Total): 850ms. Time to First Token (TTFT): 150ms. The key takeaway is how drastically context data (like files or large datasets) increases input tokens compared to simple instructions. If you call this function multiple times with the same context, you might be wasting tokens. Caching or optimizing the prompt structure becomes vital here. By refining instructions (which are smaller in scope) vs reducing context (which is large), you can manage your token budget effectively. The ratio of input to output tokens clearly dictates the performance characteristic of the specific API call.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fefce8'/><rect x='50' y='40' width='120' height='100' fill='#fef08a' fill-opacity='0.3'/><rect x='230' y='40' width='120' height='100' fill='#fef08a' fill-opacity='0.3'/><text x='110' y='90' text-anchor='middle' font-size='16'>2050 In</text><text x='290' y='90' text-anchor='middle' font-size='16'>300 Out</text><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12' fill='#a16207'>Prompt vs Output Ratio</text></svg>
studentPrompts: If you have a document with 10k tokens, but you only need to reference 500 tokens of it, how can you reduce cost?
Based on the analysis, how does increasing the output token limit affect overall cost and latency?
Create a sample scenario using AI Studio and calculate the estimated token usage before running.

slides.title: From Scratch: Context Heavy
slides.content: Developing a project directly from scratch often necessitates massive token expenditure during the initial parts. Since the AI lacks any prior knowledge of your project's specific conventions, library preferences, or structural paradigms, you are usually forced to provide comprehensive context, documentation, and explicit definitions repeatedly within every prompt. When you start building from a blank slate, you're not just asking for code; you're also embedding a tremendous amount of 'metadata' about your desired architecture. If you're building a full-stack feature from scratch, you might need to re-prompt the model to import your custom base classes, adhere to specific naming conventions defined in another file, or follow project-specific error handling strategies. This persistent re-contextualization means that for every single prompt turn, you are consuming significant input tokens just to keep the AI aligned with your requirements. Consequently, the input token volume scales rapidly with the complexity of your application compared to more constrained environments.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fff7ed'/><path d='M50 50 L350 50 L350 150 L50 150 Z' fill='none' stroke='#f97316' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='10 5'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#9a3412'>High Context Overhead (From Scratch)</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Explain why context overhead is generally higher in blank-slate projects.
How does defining project conventions upfront help in reducing future token consumption?

slides.title: Bootstrapping: Optimized Structure
slides.content: In contrast, scaffolding or bootstrapping a project upon a mature framework or a CMS drastically reduces individual prompt token complexity. In these environments, the structural patterns, base classes, routing systems, and core components are either already defined or can be easily referenced through established paradigms. When you prompt the model within such an environment, your instructions can be much more concise, focusing only on the specific business logic or feature you need to implement, because the model already 'understands' (or can easily look up) the existing codebase context. The framework or CMS itself acts as a 'token multiplier'—it provides implicit instructions and structural guardrails without needing explicit re-statement in your prompts. Instead of describing an entire database schema from scratch, you might just refer to a 'model that extends BaseRepository'. This optimization not only lowers the input token consumption per prompt but also significantly increases the 'semantic density' of your interactions, as more of your token budget is allocated to actual productive output rather than logistical re-alignment.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f0fdfa'/><path d='M50 50 L350 50 L350 150 L50 150 Z' fill='none' stroke='#059669' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#065f46'>Scaffolded Structure (Frameworks/CMS)</text></svg>
studentPrompts: How does scaffolding or using a CMS reduce the need for repeat instructions?
Compare the token consumption of a simple logic add-on to a framework vs. the same feature from scratch.
